claim: For all integers n > 1, n is prime if and only if (n-1)! + 1 is divisible by n.
If n is prime, then (n-1)! ≡ -1 (mod n). (Wilson's theorem)
If n is composite and n > 4, then (n-1)! ≡ 0 (mod n).
For prime p, the multiplicative group (ℤ/pℤ)* is cyclic of order p-1.
For prime p, there exists a primitive root g modulo p.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (any(pow(g, n-1, n) == 1 and all(pow(g, (n-1)//q, n) != 1 for q in [2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13] if (n-1) % q == 0) for g in range(1, n)))

claim: For composite n > 4, every prime divisor p of n divides (n-1)!.
predicate: (is_prime(n)) or (n <= 4) or (all((math.factorial(n-1) % p == 0) for p in range(2, n) if n % p == 0))

claim: For all n > 1, either (n-1)! ≡ -1 (mod n) or (n-1)! ≡ 0 (mod n).
For composite n = p·q with distinct primes p, q, both p-1 and q-1 divide n-1.
predicate: (n <= 1) or (is_prime(n)) or (not any(n % (p*q) == 0 and is_prime(p) and is_prime(q) and p != q for p in range(2, n) for q in range(p+1, n)))

claim: For prime p > 2, (p-1)/2 has the same parity as the number of quadratic residues mod p.
For n = 4, we have (n-1)! + 1 = 7, which is not divisible by 4.
4 is the only composite number n > 1 such that (n-1)! + 1 is not divisible by n.
If n is odd and composite, then (n-1)! is even.
If n is composite, then vp(n, p) ≤ vp((n-1)!, p) for every prime p dividing n.
predicate: (is_prime(n)) or (all(vp(n, p) <= vp(math.factorial(n-1), p) for p in range(2, n) if n % p == 0))

claim: For odd prime p, (p-1)! ≡ -1 (mod p) and (p-1)! is even.
For odd prime p, phi(p) = p - 1 is even.
For prime p, the product of all nonzero residues modulo p equals -1 (mod p).
For composite n > 4, the product of all residues 1,...,n-1 is divisible by n.
For odd prime p, (p-1)/2 is a positive integer.
For odd prime p, (p-1)! has odd prime factors and is even.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (n == 2) or ((math.factorial(n-1) % 2) == 0 and any((math.factorial(n-1) % q) == 0 for q in [3, 5, 7, 11, 13] if q < n))

claim: For all primes p, p divides (p-1)! + 1.
For prime p, (p-1)! + 1 ≡ 0 (mod p), so (p-1)! + 1 is a multiple of p.
For all n > 4, if n is composite then n divides (n-1)!.
If n = p² for prime p ≥ 3, then p divides (n-1)! with multiplicity at least 2.
predicate: (n % (n // int(abs(n) ** 0.5 + 0.5) if int(abs(n) ** 0.5 + 0.5) ** 2 == n else 1) != 0) or (vp(math.factorial(n-1), int(abs(n) ** 0.5 + 0.5)) >= 2)

claim: For prime p, the set {1, 2, ..., p-1} forms a group under multiplication modulo p.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or all(gcd((i * j) % n, n) == 1 for i in range(1, n) for j in range(1, n))

claim: For prime p, at least one primitive root exists modulo p.
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (any(pow(g, k, n) != 1 for k in range(1, n-1) for g in range(1, n) if pow(g, n-1, n) == 1))

claim: For composite n > 4 with smallest prime factor p, we have vp((n-1)!, p) ≥ vp(n, p) + 1.
predicate: (is_prime(n)) or (n <= 4) or (all(vp(math.factorial(n-1), p) >= vp(n, p) + 1 for p in range(2, n) if n % p == 0))

claim: For all primes p, the number (p-1)! + 1 is a positive multiple of p.
For prime p, (p-1)! + 1 ≥ p.
For odd prime p, p-1 is even and (p-1)! satisfies (p-1)! ≡ -1 (mod p).
For odd prime p, ((p-1)/2)! is a quadratic residue or non-residue depending on p ≡ 1 or 3 (mod 4).
For odd prime p, (p-1)! is even and (p-1)! ≡ -1 (mod p).
For odd prime p, there exists an even number k < p such that k·(p-1)! ≡ -k (mod p).
predicate: (not is_prime(n)) or (n == 2) or (any((k * math.factorial(n-1)) % n == (n - k) % n for k in range(2, n, 2)))

claim: The set of composite numbers n > 4 such that (n-1)! + 1 is divisible by n is precisely the set of all composite n > 4.
For composite n with all prime factors ≤ √n, we have (n-1)! divisible by n.
predicate: (is_prime(n)) or (all(p <= int(abs(n) ** 0.5) + 1 for p in range(2, n) if n % p == 0)) or ((math.factorial(n-1) % n) == 0)

claim: For odd prime p, phi(p) = p - 1 > 1 and is even.
For odd prime p, there are at least (p-1)/2 - 1 > 0 elements of order dividing (p-1)/2 in (ℤ/pℤ)*.
For prime p ≥ 5, (p-1)! + 1 ≥ 2p.
For all n > 4, n is prime if and only if (n-1)! is not divisible by n.
For all n > 4, n is composite if and only if (n-1)! ≡ 0 (mod n).
For odd prime p, the product of all nonzero residues modulo p is congruent to -1.
For all n ≥ 5, n is prime if and only if (n-1)! is not divisible by n, and n is composite if and only if (n-1)! is divisible by n.
For composite n = p·q with distinct odd primes p < q, we have vp((n-1)!, p) ≥ 2·vp(n, p).

claim: For odd composite n > 4, (n-1)! is even and divisible by n.
For odd composite n > 4, (n-1)! is divisible by 2n.
For all primes p ≥ 5, the quotient ((p-1)! + 1) / p is at least 2.
For prime p ≥ 7, ((p-1)! + 1) / p > p - 2.
For composite n > 4, every prime p < n that divides n also divides (n-1)!.
